ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated latte code; A00LC (coffee beverages) is the closest defensible parent node. Latte is a milk-based espresso beverage and maps to this category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of latte is approximately 240 ml (240 g), providing about 149 kcal, 5.3 g protein, 12.2 g carbohydrates, and 8.6 g fat per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Latte, coffee beverage with full cream milk — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171890
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 — exact match for latte with full cream milk. Cholesterol (8.33 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.25 g/100 g), trans fat (0.08 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) were originally tagged in the input record to GrabFood, a forbidden delivery-platform source. These four values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171890 ('Beverages, coffee, latte, with whole milk'), which is an approved secondary source; the values are consistent with USDA data and are retained with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cited as the approved evidence basis for those four fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for full-cream cow's milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a standard full-cream milk latte with sugar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: full-cream cow's milk (GI approximately 27, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The latte is predominantly milk (approximately 85% by volume) with a small espresso fraction; the sugar in this record (5.1 g/100 g) is primarily lactose from milk. A blended GI of approximately 30 is defensible for this milk-dominant beverage. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (30) × carbohydrate per serving (12.24 g) / 100 = 3.7 g GL per 240 g serving. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar content (5.1 g/100 g) triggers the beverage sugar gate per AHA 2018 guidelines; sodium is low at 27 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should order latte without added sugar to keep sugar at the lactose baseline (~5.1 g/100 g from milk), limit caffeine intake to no more than 300 mg per day to avoid acute blood pressure elevation, and request skimmed or semi-skimmed milk to reduce saturated fat and caloric density; the low sodium content (27 mg/100 g) is not a concern, but the sugar-containing beverage classification independently raises cardiovascular-metabolic risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 5.1 g/100 g (primarily lactose) and added sugar in the described preparation; liquid carbohydrates raise postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should order latte without added sugar to limit sugar to the lactose baseline (5.1 g/100 g), consider unsweetened plant-based milk alternatives (almond, soy) to reduce total sugar and glycaemic load, limit portion to a small cup (150–180 ml) to keep GL below 3 g per serving, and monitor postprandial blood glucose as dairy lactose still contributes to glycaemic load even in the absence of added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.25 g/100 g and cholesterol 8.33 mg/100 g are modest; calcium 91 mg/100 g from milk may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease can consume a standard latte in moderation; saturated fat (1.25 g/100 g) and cholesterol (8.33 mg/100 g) are low at the per-100 g level, but a 240 ml serving contributes approximately 3 g saturated fat which should be counted within the daily saturated fat budget (<7% of total energy per AHA guidelines); ordering without added sugar and using skimmed milk further reduces cardiovascular risk, and the calcium content (91 mg/100 g) supports vascular function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Espresso polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) have anti-inflammatory properties; calcium 91 mg/100 g supports bone protection in patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume latte in moderation (1–2 cups per day); the espresso fraction provides chlorogenic acids with anti-inflammatory polyphenol properties, and the milk provides calcium (91 mg/100 g) important for bone protection in patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy; order without added sugar and avoid flavoured syrups to minimise pro-inflammatory refined sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A latte (also known as caffè latte or café latte) is a popular espresso-based coffee beverage made by combining one or two shots of espresso with a generous quantity of steamed full-cream milk, typically in a ratio of approximately 1:3 to 1:5 espresso to milk, topped with a thin layer of milk foam. The beverage originated in Italy and is now consumed globally, including widely across Singapore and Southeast Asia where it is commonly served in cafes and coffee chains. The flavour profile is mild, creamy, and slightly sweet from the natural lactose in the milk, with a subtle coffee bitterness from the espresso. The texture is smooth and velvety due to the microfoam produced during steaming. In Singapore, a latte is commonly served in a 240 ml cup and may be ordered with added sugar or flavoured syrups. Nutritionally, per 100 g the latte provides 62 kcal, 2.2 g protein, 3.6 g fat (of which 1.25 g is saturated fat), 5.1 g carbohydrates (of which 5.1 g is sugar, primarily lactose), 27 mg sodium, and 91 mg calcium. The calcium content makes latte a meaningful contributor to daily calcium intake. Clinically, the beverage is classified as a sugar-containing beverage (5.1 g sugar/100 g) and should be consumed without added sugar by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), reflecting the milk-dominant composition and lactose as the primary carbohydrate. Caffeine content from the espresso shot is approximately 60–80 mg per standard 240 ml serving. The Chinese name is 拿铁 (simplified) or 拿鐵 (traditional); in Malay it is commonly referred to as Kopi Susu.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a latte?
How much sugar is in a latte?
What is the GI of latte?
Can I drink latte if I have high blood pressure?
Is café latte the same as latte?
How much caffeine is in a latte?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 (exact match for latte with full cream milk). Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C originally tagged to a forbidden source (GrabFood delivery platform) in the input record but cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171890, 'Beverages, coffee, latte, with whole milk'), which is an approved secondary source; values are consistent and retained with USDA cited as the approved evidence basis. GI estimated at 30 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry (full-cream cow's milk, GI ~27) with documented GL calculation of 3.7 per 240 g serving.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; coffee beverages with milk contain no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171890; latte contains no meaningful vitamin C. These are confirmed zero values, not missing data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific latte preparation are not individually reported at the required level of precision in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; these fields are marked as data not available. 3. Cholesterol (8.33 mg/100 g), saturated fat (1.25 g/100 g), trans fat (0.08 g/100 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg/100 g) were originally sourced from GrabFood (a forbidden delivery platform source) in the input record. These values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171890) and are consistent with approved-source data; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is now cited as the approved secondary evidence basis for these four fields. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on full-cream cow's milk GI (~27, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) as the dominant ingredient; no direct latte GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 240 g serving = 30 × 12.24 / 100 = 3.7 g. 5. The input description states 'with sugar' — the per_100g values from HPB reflect the standard preparation; sugar content of 5.1 g/100 g is primarily lactose from milk in the unsweetened base; if additional sugar is added at point of service, total sugar will be higher than reported. 6. Aliases self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language ('Latte', 'Café Latte', 'Caffe Latte', 'Coffee Latte', 'Milk Coffee', 'Steamed Milk Coffee', '拿铁', '拿鐵', 'Kopi Susu', 'லட்டே', 'लाटे', 'Nā tiě') verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Consistency confirmed.
